exemplar.title: What is the effect of CO2 emissions from vehicles on the abundance of species (Leucophyllum frutescens and Conocarpus erectus) next to the roads in high (Juffair) and low-traffic areas (Bu Quwah)?

detailedExemplarData.title: What is the effect of CO2 emissions from vehicles on the abundance of species (Leucophyllum frutescens and Conocarpus erectus) next to the roads in high (Juffair) and low-traffic areas (Bu Quwah)?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Use of appropriate subject-specific terminology (ppm, standard deviation, urban heat island)
Overall structure follows IB IA conventions with clear section headings
weaknesses: Tables and figures sometimes interrupt the narrative flow; headings break over pages
Formatting inconsistencies in bibliography and figure captions reduce coherence
Verbose sections and repetitive background material affect concision and readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure is present but not consistently sustained (band 2), terminology usage is correct but report lacks concision (band 2), and logic/coherence is acceptable but interrupted at points (band 2), justifying a score of 2.

strengths: Exceptionally focused research question specifying COâ, species names and high-/low-traffic comparison
Rich, localized context using national car-ownership statistics and urban-heat-island considerations
Clear linkage to sustainable transportation policy and Bahrainâs arid climate
weaknesses: Explanation of how other urban factors (irrigation regimes, soil salinity) interact with COâ could be more fully developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research question formulation and environmental issue context) meet the top band and the third strand (connection explanation) sits at the upper range of the mid-band. Overall performance is consistently at the 5â6 descriptor level, meriting the full 6 marks.

strengths: Repeatable, step-by-step method with detailed equipment list and sensor stabilization procedures
Thorough justification for species choice linked to prevalence and tolerance traits
weaknesses: Sampling strategy under-justified: only one quadrat per site without replication or randomization rationale
Risk assessment lacks likelihood/severity rankings and detailed mitigation steps
Insufficient data sufficiency due to only two sites and one quadrat each
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design is repeatable but insufficient for full variability analysis (band 3), sampling strategy only outlines rather than justifies choices (band 2), and the risk/ethical table outlines issues without comprehensive depth (band 3). Averaging these, the overall performance aligns with a band-3 score.

strengths: Integration of relevant literature (e.g., Nowak et al.) to contextualize unexpected patterns
Meaningful identification of method strengths and weaknesses in table form
Practical future research suggestions (spatial scale extension, botanist consultation, timing standardization)
weaknesses: Evaluation of conclusion lacks quantitative ecological significance metrics
Discussion of method limitations does not detail how each weakness influenced data magnitude
Improvement suggestions remain general rather than specifying new methodological designs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusion evaluation and method evaluation each meet the mid-band descriptor but lack depth; improvements are meaningful but not significant. All three strands align with band-3 performance, supporting a score of 3.

strengths: Data are presented in clearly labeled tables and two graphs with error bars
Processed data use correct formulas and clear LaTeX formatting
Correct calculation of means and standard deviations
weaknesses: One graph lacks clear axis units; species-abundance graph is missing
No inferential statistical tests to support significance of trends
Inconsistent use of significant figures and incomplete uncertainty propagation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation is appropriate with minor omissions (band 4). Data analysis is correct but incomplete without statistical tests (band 3). Conclusion interpretation is partially supported (band 3). Overall, the stronger data presentation justifies a score of 4 in this criterion.

strengths: Application proposal links to findings by recommending tolerant species as pollution filters
Identifies key strengths (filtration, micro-climate benefits) and weaknesses (water scarcity, maintenance)
weaknesses: Justification of the solution is general and not tightly based on data
Evaluation omits critical trade-offs such as cost analysis and biodiversity impacts
Resource requirements and maintenance costs are not evaluated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposal describes a solution with weak justification (band 2) and evaluates some strengths and weaknesses but not comprehensively (band 2), warranting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific terms (ppm, standard deviation, urban heat island) are used correctly, but verbosity and repetition reduce concision.  Therefore the strand meets either terminology or concision (terminology) but not both, aligning with band 2.

The discussion relates findings to the wider issue (traffic pollution, urban planting) and acknowledges contradictions, but evaluation depth is patchy (e.g., no quantitative estimate of ecological significance).  Omissions prevent full coverage, hence band 3-4 with a score of 3.

The conclusion restates trends (higher COâ in Juffair, unexpected higher abundance) and references data values, but support is limited by the small dataset and no inferential stats.  Interpretation is therefore partially valid and fits the mid-band.

Means and standard deviations are calculated correctly, yet no statistical test of difference or correlation is performed and only COâ data are analysed quantitatively.  Analysis is basically correct but incomplete, matching the 3-4 descriptor; the lower value in the band is awarded.

The student proposes extending spatial scale, consulting botanists, standardising times and measuring more pollutants.  These modifications address key weaknesses but remain general (no specific methodological redesign).  Suggestions are âmeaningfulâ rather than âsignificantâ, meeting the 3-4 band.

Step-by-step procedures, equipment list and sensor stabilisation details make the method repeatable; however, using only two sites and a single quadrat per site cannot yield âsufficientâ data to address variability.  Thus the method is appropriate but insufficient, placing it mid-band (3).

Connections between traffic COâ, physiological plant stress and expected changes in abundance are explained, but other variables (e.g. differential planting regimes, irrigation) are only acknowledged later.  The explanation is sound yet not completely comprehensive, so the strand fits the 3-4 band rather than the top.

The student states a clear, narrowly-focused comparative question that specifies the pollutant (COâ), the dependent variable (abundance of two named species) and the spatial context (high- and low-traffic areas).  Wording is precise enough to guide data collection and analysis, with no ambiguity about what will be measured or compared; therefore the descriptor for a "relevant, coherent and focused" RQ is met.

Strengths (pollution filtration, micro-climate) and weaknesses (water scarcity, maintenance) are mentioned, but not all relevant trade-offs (cost, biodiversity impacts) are explored.  Hence partial evaluation earns 2 marks.

The report follows the IB IA structure with clear headings, but tables occasionally break over pages and some figures lack captions.  Organisation is present though not fully sustained, fitting band 2.

A strengths/weaknesses table identifies precision sensors, replication, limited spatial scale, species ID bias, etc.âcovering both positives and limitations.  Discussion lacks detail on how each limitation influenced data magnitude, so the descriptor for a thorough âdiscussionâ is not fully reached.

The introduction discusses vehicle-derived greenhouse gases, cites national car-ownership statistics, outlines physiological plant effects, urban-heat-island considerations and Bahrainâs arid climate.  This breadth and localisation give rich context directly linked to the RQ, fulfilling the requirement for a relevant environmental issue that frames the study.

The student indicates the choice of one high- and one low-traffic road but gives no justification of quadrat size, replication, or randomisation.  Errors of omission remain, so the response only âoutlinesâ a sampling strategy.

A risk-ethical-environmental table identifies traffic danger, privacy, and waste disposal, yet depth is limited (no likelihood ranking, mitigation detail is generic).  This meets the descriptor for an outline rather than a comprehensive description.

All raw and processed numerical data are in clearly labelled tables and two relevant graphs with error bars.  Minor omissions exist (axis units on one figure slightly unclear, speciesâabundance graph absent), so the work is âappropriate with some omissionsâ.

The urban-greening proposal is described and linked to findings, yet justification relies largely on general arguments (tolerant species as filters) with limited data tie-in.  This aligns with a âdescribes with weak justificationâ performance.

Most sections progress logically, yet the evaluation tables interrupt narrative flow and some arguments jump between ideas.  Overall coherence is acceptable but not seamless, positioning the work in band 2.

annotations.comment: Conclusion restates trends but lacks inferential support; without statistical testing, claims of significance remain tentative.
Include an evaluation of resource requirements and maintenance costs in the solution evaluation to assess feasibility.
The bibliography shows a breadth of sources but formatting is inconsistent; apply a uniform citation style (e.g., APA) for coherence.

The research question is exceptionally focused, specifying the pollutant (COâ), the species names, and the comparison between high- and low-traffic areas, ensuring clarity for subsequent data collection.
The aim statement largely restates the research question, creating redundancy. Consider streamlining the aim to focus on objectives beyond repeating the RQ.
The use of national carâownership statistics provides strong contextual grounding for vehicle emissions in Bahrain, effectively linking background to the studyâs locale.
Paragraphs in the introduction are lengthy, which can impede reader engagement. Break into shorter sections with clear subâheadings to improve flow.
Consider elaborating how other urban factors (e.g., irrigation regimes, soil salinity) interact with COâ to influence plant abundance, strengthening the link to your RQ.
The sampling strategy (one quadrat per site) is underâjustified. Explain your choice of quadrat size, replication, and randomisation to justify representativeness.
The connection to sustainable transportation policy is well articulated, highlighting the studyâs broader relevance to urban planning in Bahrain.
This section repeats some background points. Consider consolidating points on pollutant mixtures to avoid redundancy.
The hypothesis is clearly stated and testable, linking COâ concentration differences to expected plant abundance changes.
Equipment list lacks precision on sensor calibration frequency and measurement intervals. Add details to ensure method repeatability.
Risk assessment table outlines relevant hazards but omits likelihood and severity rankings; include these to deepen the risk evaluation.
Provide more specific mitigation steps for each risk (e.g., traffic control measures) rather than generic statements.
The stepwise methodology is repeatable, but only two sites with one quadrat each limit data sufficiency to capture natural variability.
Justification of species choice is thorough, linking prevalence and tolerance traits to study objectives.
Figure captions describe species but lack scale bars or location markers; include these to improve clarity.
Uncertainty discussion quantifies sensor error well but does not propagate these through to the mean and SD calculations; add uncertainty propagation.
Summing height measurement uncertainties linearly may overestimate error; consider rootâsumâsquare for combined standard uncertainty.
Raw data table is clearly presented, but absence of recording times for each trial limits interpretation of diurnal COâ fluctuations.
Include a graph of species abundance per site for immediate visual comparison alongside COâ graphs.
Processed data section uses correct formulas in LaTeX, supporting clarity in calculations of mean and SD.
Statistical significance is not tested; perform a tâtest or nonparametric test to support claims about differences between sites.
Sample calculations are detailed, but significantâfigure usage is inconsistent; standardize rounding conventions across results.
Figure 3 shows error bars but omits clear axis units; ensure each graph labels its axes with units for unambiguous interpretation.
Figure 4 lacks a legend distinguishing Juffair and Bu Quwah; add color or marker keys to improve readability.
Discussion integrates relevant literature well, drawing on Nowak et al. to explain unexpected patternsâa solid evaluative element.
The discussion acknowledges limitations but does not quantify ecological significance; consider including metrics such as IPCC vulnerability indices.
Future research suggestions are relevant and practical; extending spatial scale and consulting botanists will strengthen study rigor.
Application proposal outlines phased greening well but justification is general; directly link observed species tolerance data to species selection.
Reference entries lack consistency in date formats and journal abbreviations; ensure each entry includes author, date, title, and retrieval details uniformly.
